summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's latest adjustments to its compliance plan with the Netherlands ACM's regulations on dating app payments. Apple made three changes: removing the separate binary requirement, updating payment service provider criteria, and softening the consumer disclosure language. Gruber views all changes as improvements, particularly the warning screen language which moved from implying only Apple's system is secure to more neutral, factual phrasing. He also notes Apple's separate announcement allowing 'reader' apps to link to external websites for account management per their Japan JFTC agreement. Gruber characterizes these as baby steps that strategically reduce regulatory pressure, concluding that Apple is stubborn but pragmatic.
key_points: Apple dropped the requirement for a separate Netherlands-only binary, which would have confused users and complicated updates for existing app installations
The updated warning screen language shifted from implying only Apple's payment system is secure to a more neutral, factual tone about what users will lose
Apple still hasn't clarified what the 'Learn More' link in the external payment warning actually does
Apple's 'reader' app entitlement allowing links to external websites for account management went live, fulfilling the Japan JFTC agreement
All these incremental changes serve Apple's strategy of making just enough concessions to decrease regulatory pressure
money_quotes: None of these proposed changes are earth-shattering, but they're all for the better.

Apple's initial proposal that apps using these entitlements in the Netherlands would need to do so using a separate binary — meaning, a second Netherlands-only version of the app — seemed deliberately confusing.
The old title sort of implied not just that Apple's payment system is private and secure, but that only Apple's payment system is private and secure.
The new language is more matter-of-fact, which feels appropriate.
As far as I can tell, though, Apple still hasn't said what's supposed to happen if a user taps on 'Learn More'.
Baby steps, but all of these changes are in the direction of decreasing regulatory pressure.
Apple can be stubborn but they're not stupid.
key_insight: Apple's incremental concessions on App Store payment rules reveal a pragmatic strategy of yielding just enough to relieve regulatory pressure while preserving as much control as possible.
